sentences.text: My name is Penina, P-E-N-I-N-A, Weisz, W-E-I-S-Z, Bowman, B-O-W-M-A-N.
At birth, it was Pszi, P-S-Z-I, Weisz.
I also have-- I was also named in Hungarian Piroska, in Romanian Paraskiva, and then Piri for short for, for Piroska, Pszi in Yiddish, and then Penina in Hebrew, and Penny in English.
I was born April 19, 1927.
Today is 68.
I was born in Cluj, Romania.
Yes.
I had two sisters and one brother.
The oldest was Yaffa.
She was born in 1923.
She's, she's 70-something. 1923, she was born in '23.
My brother, Mordechai, was born in 1925.
I was born in 1927.
And my youngest sister, Miriam, was born in 1929.
Mother and father, and we were all born in Cluj, Romania.
We were raised very religious.
We were extreme Orthodox.
My father was a ritual bath owner.
They called it a mikvah.
And we, we had to be an example in the community because-- we had to observe all the laws, and we were raised very religious.
We observed all the holidays.
Passover, we had separate dishes.
And us girls were not allowed to wear short sleeves.
We used to cheat and roll them up when we were away from the house.
And we were not allowed to wear bobby socks.
We had to wear long stockings.
On the Sabbath, we were, we were not allowed to tear paper or use money or walk too far.
And of course, we were extreme kosher, only kosher food.
And we went to Jewish schools.
And we were not allowed to date.
We cheated with that.
We were not allowed to be Zionists.
No.
Religious Jews were not allowed to be Zionists.
They were supposed to wait for the Messiah to come.
And my father forbade us to be Zionists, but the Zionist youth movement, the general Zionist youth movement was very strong in our city.
And we all secretly belonged to it.
And my oldest sister was very active in it.
We were still a little bit too young.
But she used to go to meetings regularly.
And my father forbade her to go.
And sometimes she would, we would prop up the pillows in the bed, pretend that my sister was under covers sleeping.
And she would sneak out.
My father locked the doors, made sure she didn't go out.
But she would sneak out through the window to go to a Zionist youth group meeting.
And she even had certificates to go to Palestine in 1938, but my father forbade her from going.
And her friends all went, and they survived the war and live in Palestine.
Well, we were, we were very Zionist-oriented.
And we children, we used to go to the meetings-- not as much for the younger ones.
My sister is four years older.

So we used to go to the meetings and learned the songs and sang and hoped for that in the future we will be settling in Palestine, that we will make aliyah one of these days, and we will establish a kibbutz.
So we were always with that hope and that we will someday will go and learned all the songs and, and used to take classes to study a little bit the history of, of Palestine.
As children, our hobbies-- our main hobbies was walking.
We used to go to the park a lot and play.
And reading, that was one of the main hobbies.
I did a little gardening and learned a little cooking.
But the reading was-- we were all passionate readers.
We used to save our little pennies and go to the library just to borrow, because you couldn't borrow books like you do today and get them free.
We had to borrow books and pay a few pennies for it.
So we used to take out books, and we were all curled up in bed and read.
That was our most popular pastime.
In some ways, yes.
We, we used to go to synagogue regularly, and my parents every day and Shabbat.
And we observed the holidays very intensely.
And we were all very oriented toward, toward observance.
But we cheated.
[LAUGHS]
Yes.
As children we all enjoyed it.
We rebelled against certain customs and ceremonies.
We thought it was too much.
Well, like, we couldn't be Zionists and we couldn't date, you know, couldn't-- and on the Sabbath we couldn't walk too far, because that was considered labor, and we never liked that.
And we, we were not allowed to, for instance, to wear slacks, because women should not imitate a man's form.
And so that was like idolatry.
You couldn't take pictures, and you couldn't wear slacks, you know, so we always cheated.
And we used to take it with us and then change when we were away from house.
We couldn't pursue any modern sports.
Like, you couldn't go ice skating or skiing or do anything like that.
So we, we used to cheat with that.
Well, it slowly built up during the-- we were under Romanian rule until 1940.
And then when the Hungarians took over, certain restrictions started happening slowly.
In 1940 and '41, and slowly, we weren't allowed to go to synagogue.
And you weren't allowed on the streets until, until 8:00 in the morning so--
Right.
Oh, we, we heard rumors that the Jews, Jews were being taken to camp, to labor camps.
And my parents-- we never heard anything about Auschwitz or concentration or anything like that.
Now, parents used to whisper to each other about things happening.
But we children never heard any mention of that, just that the Jewish people, we should get our rucksacks together, because we don't know when we will be taken to camps to work.
And my father always said that, you know, not to worry about that.
My sister even had an opportunity by a Romanian family to hide us.
And he said, we're not worried about going to camp.
Jewish people are not afraid of work.
Everybody has to suffer during the war.
Some people have to be soldiers and fight on the fronts, and some people have to work in factories, and we are not afraid to go to work.
So we, we never heard-- definitely we heard people were being taken away by transport, but we never heard where or--
It was, because we, we were, you know-- just from day to day, we didn't know when we were going to be taken away.
But we prepared by having, like, a backpack and prepare, you know, some of our, our clothing that we could get to it fast.
But we never, we never, you know-- we weren't scared every minute of the day.
Well, into Hungary-- in the later years, when, when, the I guess-- we weren't taken-- Hungarian Jews were not taken until April of 1944.
So the restrictions started, you know, a couple of years before with curfew and the schools, not being able to go to the school that we went to.
And our house was taken away.
So little things were happening, but not bad enough to fear for our lives.

Well, the, the Hungarian soldiers came and said, we need this house, because it was located across from the synagogue, and it was a location in the house, they had-- it had a cellar, and it had, in the same courtyard, we had a, a salami factory.
And they wanted that.
So we were evicted, and we were told to move out and took over our house.
So we moved like, just about four or five blocks away to another location.
That was in-- it, it was probably late 1943, because in April of '44, we were taken.
Oh, we were-- we lived in Cluj, and, and by that time-- Romania became Hungary in 1940, so it was called Kolozsvar, the name of the city.
And we-- the Hungarian soldiers knocked on our door.
And they said to get ready in 20 minutes, that we will take you to work and to take with us clothing and food and broomsticks.
Because they thought that we were going to work, and we were going to have to clean up, so whatever broomstick we had.
So we, we started, you know, running in every direction, trying to figure out what to take.
And we had 20 minutes.
And my father-- we all started crying.
And my mother was in the middle of, of making bread that morning, and my father was in the middle of praying with his prayer shawl on him.
So he just said now to calm down and to just take what's, you know, best for us, to keep our minds and, and our heads straight and get the clothes and the food that we can take with us.
And we, you know, we tried to take our souvenirs, and we couldn't.
We used to have these beautiful awards from school.
They were like little tins if you did, you know, well in school, gold tins.
So we wanted to take them, and they said, no, you can't take any pictures or anything else.
Just take food and warm clothing.
So we all put on several layers of clothing, and we were told to go out to the courtyard in 20 minutes or else.
And the soldier just, you know, showed his gun.
So we knew very well what he meant to say.
So we had to obey.
And we-- How old were you at that time?
I was 17.
Yes.
So he marched us out in the courtyard, and we waited until everybody was gathered from that immediate community, and we were the very first street in Kolozsvar to be taken.
And I remember I didn't have my good walking shoes, so I went over to the soldier and asked him if I could go get my good shoes, because I just had sandals.
And he said, you can go get it from the shoemaker, which was just down the block.
But I'll have to walk with you so you will not escape.
So he walked with me down to the shoemaker to get my shoes.
And I came back.
My family was lined up in the courtyard.
And we all marched about two or three miles to the outskirts of the city, which was a, a brick factory.
And that's where we were gathered to be taken to camp.
So we stayed in this brick factory for about-- oh, about two weeks and waited for transport to be taken to work camp.
For about two weeks.
We, we were, we were terribly crowded.

And every family received just, like, a corner, so we were, like, in a room about this size, an average room size, maybe 12 by 12, and we each-- a family had to, to lie down and make room, you know, just for about eight or 10 people in this small area.
So we children-- we were four children and my mother and father, and we just got a corner in this little factory.
And we stayed there, and we, we tried to make the best of it.
We sang songs, and we, we flirted with the boys.
We were all, you know, young girls, 15, 17.
And my brother was 19, my sister 21.
So we just sang songs and, and prayed and, and played games and tried to make the best of it.
And we couldn't wait to be taken already so that-- because we thought, now, nothing can be worse than this, you know, to live on a floor in such temporary headquarters so--
We had taken food from home.
And we did not get any food from outside.
We had taken food from home.
People brought food, and some of the outsiders-- I don't know who they were, but they brought some additional food.
We were in this, in this brick factory about two weeks.
Then, we were lined up and taken to trains.
And the trains, we were-- we didn't know where we were going.
And the trains-- we were-- we kept looking at as much as we could see from the windows.
Cattle cars, just regular cattle cars.
And one of the things that my mother had taken on the trip that we had left was onions.
And that was almost, like, what made us survive, because we were munching on onions, and that kind of woke you up, you know.
It was kind of a strong taste.
But we were on this train ride for days.
I don't remember how many days.
And then some people, some people died on the train.
We didn't, we didn't have, you know, food and water, and, and the conditions were terrible.
And I remember specifically one old man with a beard that died, and our parents told us don't turn that way and just not to look in that direction.
So when the train ride was over, we found ourselves in Auschwitz.
Well, we were, you know, we were scared, and we were worried, and everybody praying and wondering what's going to happen and where we were going, but we still didn't know where we were going.
We thought we were going to work, but we just couldn't figure out how we could be in such terrible conditions.
If we were going to work, you know, how are we going to survive if we don't get food and water and, and starvation?
There were neighbors, some of the neighbors from our community.
And my mother, my father, and my, my, you know, the two sisters and brother.
We were all together.
And there were several neighbors that were in the same car, yes.
And we got to Auschwitz, right.
No.
Never.
Never heard of it.
When we got there, we saw the sign that said, 'Arbeit macht frei.' And I remember reading it, because we knew-- our Yiddish was very similar to German.
And we read it and said, gee, it can't be that bad.
It says work makes you free.
So if you work hard, you're going to be free.
So we just thought we were going to work.
And we arrived there.
And immediately we were pushed, you know, to the left and to the right.
And my, my mother was pushed to one side with the older people, even though she was just 40-- she was 47.
And 47 in those days was very old.

So she was pushed to one side and-- with, with older people, and young people who were carrying their children on their arms, because the Germans didn't want to separate the mother from the child and create a scene.
So they just pushed the young people who were carrying little babies to one side with the older people.
And my brother and my father went to another side.
And my two sisters and I went to one side where the working people went.
Well, you know, it was just mobs of people and soldiers all around us.
And then the Polish workers with the striped uniforms were, were shouting orders.
And, and the Germans with the guns standing by some of the soldiers.
And I think one of the soldiers later, we thought-- my sister and I thought that it was Mengele because he wore the black shiny boots and with his cane.

And he was directing traffic and telling which person should go on which side, which in essence was who will live and who will die, because we didn't know that but he was picking the able-bodied, nice looking to one side and then the frail-looking, the older person and very young to the other side.

And they were just standing there, and just everything was very fast, you know, just shouting and mobs and people crying and-- Well, next thing we know, we were out of that line, and we were marched to another location, which was-- we were both in C Lager  and B Lager.
So one of them was-- the first lager was, I think, C Lager, and we were in B Lager.
And then we ended up in C Lager, which one lager was where the crematorium ovens were and the gas chambers.
And then people were selected from there and then they were sent to the other camp.
Well, we didn't find out for the longest time.
We, we were marched to a barracks, and then we were told to undress.
And the first person went to one room, and the person first cut our hair off, first with the scissor.
And then we went to the next room, and then they shaved us all, both our hair and under the arm and the pubic hair, everyplace.
And we were told that for sanitary reasons.
And my sister was standing next to me and was yelling my name, Pszi, where are you?
And, and I was right next to her, and did not-- she did not recognize me, because I, I was-- I had waist-length hair, and here I was shaven.
She didn't recognize me.
So from there, you know, we went to the barracks.
And we lived in the barracks for, for weeks before-- we really never realized what was happening, I mean, in Auschwitz.
Really?
No.
And we were-- we were like zombies.
You know, as soon as you arrived, you were-- you changed clothes.
They gave us from a pile, just pick one dress.
So we picked like a grandmother's dress.
And we walked around.
And because of lack of food, we, we couldn't think.
And we really barely just existed.
And, and you-- the feeling was like when you go-- before you go under surgery, and you're kind of not all there.
That's how we felt most of the time [INAUDIBLE].
We heard rumors that the weak and the children and older people were being gassed and taken.
And we saw trucks go by all the time, you know, just, just packed with bodies whenever--
Yes.
They were the special black trucks, and there were bodies.
So people, you know, used to say, well, those are, you know, people who were sick and people who were probably old or couldn't work.
And we saw-- we smelled the smoke, you know, in the distance.
We smelled and saw the smoke.
And we, we heard the overseers kept saying, the only way you get out of here is through there, pointing to the sky.
But we did not believe it.
We just felt that if we--
No, we did not.
We thought the war was going to end any day.
This was already April, May of 1944.
And rumors were coming every day that, you know, that Hitler lost and the war is ending.
And we thought we were going to survive.
We were going to hold out.
We were going to be strong and clean and keep ourselves clean and--
I think it was April-- it was after Pesach in 1944.
So it was probably the end of April of 1944.
I don't know the date.
In the barracks, yes.
My sister Yaffa and Miriam and I were shoved to one side, and we were marched together, and we tried to stay together.
And we ended up in one barrack.
And this barrack was for about 800 women.
And we had three tiers, and we each had to pick a spot.
And [INAUDIBLE] got was one blanket.
And we just slept on these boards with one blanket.
We were three, three abreast, next to each other.
And then three on the next level.
Three levels.
Right.
We, we were what they called zahlappell.
We were-- a whistle would blow, and the siren would go.
And at 5 o'clock in the morning, we had to get up and line up outside to be counted.
And we were standing in line sometimes three or four hours until the Germans came to count us, the SS woman and man.
And they would just line up, five and five abreast and be counted, so it was very hard because we just stand there for three, four hours.
And later-- they didn't do anything until they counted us.
And then they dismissed us.
We'd go back in the barracks, and we just-- nothing.
We'd just, just wait for-- to get our food.
And then in the evening, they counted us again.
And basically, we-- people in Auschwitz were picked sometimes to go to work and sent out right away.
We were in Auschwitz six months, so we did not-- almost never worked.
Six months, yes.
No.
The-- in 1944 when the Hungarian Jews were picked, they killed about 400,000 Jews real fast, and they did not want to take the time to tattoo you.
And so we were-- the Hungarian Jews were just, just like 10,000 a day.
I mean, they were just killing them as fast as possible.
So we were just kind of waiting around there, either to be killed or to be shipped out-- I mean, for the war to end.
Not to be shipped out, but for the war to end.
And they didn't want to take the time to tattoo you anymore.
So we were never tattooed.
No.
About my-- we, we never-- we used to see men soldiers, men prisoners walk by every now and then.
We always looked closely to see if we knew anybody.
People would ask about their relatives, but nobody knew anything.
The only one that we saw was an aunt, [NON-ENGLISH], who was taken from Cluj, but a different time than we were.
And we met there again.
And she was in a different barracks.
But that's the only one we met.
And some school friends, but we never heard anything about our, our cousins and aunts and uncles.
We never saw them again.
We never heard anything about them until after the war.
We, we, we-- I think we survived because we were able to support each other.
And like, like, in numbers, there's strength, you know, and just-- we would encourage each other.

Every time when one was down and real depressed and the other one would say, OK, you know, just the war will end, and just, you know, hold on, and just to, to encourage each other and support each other.
When we went out we-- until we waited to be counted, we would hug each other to, to, to keep each other warm.
And before selection, my sister would pinch my cheeks to give me a little color.
So just basically, because we were together and we, we talked and we sang songs and we--
We, we prayed and we sang songs from our youth, the-- all the Zionist songs, that we were going to go to, you know, to Palestine and build the land, and all the songs.
And we would practice our Hebrew, because during our childhood, we, we studied Hebrew.
And at day school, we had to learn how to read and write, but we weren't allowed to use it as a language, because it's a holy tongue.
You're not supposed to use it for speaking.
So in camp, we were practicing our Hebrew, because we wanted-- we knew we were going to Palestine when we were liberated.
And we just kept our place clean, and we just-- and we really supported each other.
And my sister realized that people-- they had selections constantly.
And my sister saw that the selections, were not-- they weren't picking able-bodied people and real strong and healthy ones.
They were picking weak people.
So my sister said, let's not rush to the selections.
So we, we held back so the Germans would say, OK, everybody out of the barrack.
And then when everybody went out and they only took 500.
So we-- they didn't take everybody.
So we didn't rush to go out of the barrack, so we escaped many selections that way.
In Auschwitz, we had one overseer who was a teacher from Hungary, from Cluj-- Kolozsvar, the same city.
And she helped the German overseer.
And she encouraged us to keep clean and healthy.
And she also told us to go to this selection where eventually we were sent out of Auschwitz.
